expectations.text: Output contains an explicit ASVS level decision section with rationale
expectations.evidence: Section '## ASVS Level Decision' explicitly states 'Chosen Level: 2' followed by a multi-sentence rationale tying the choice to ~250k MAU, PII, GDPR, and account-takeover impact, plus a 'Feature-Level Escalations' subsection that explicitly escalates F-04 to L3-equivalent. The rationale also explains why L1 is insufficient and L3 is not justified, which is stronger than a bare assertion.

expectations.text: Chosen ASVS level is 2 (with optional escalations) — appropriate for B2C SaaS holding PII
expectations.evidence: Level 2 is chosen as the baseline, with a documented per-feature escalation: 'F-04 (Support-agent recovery): Treat as L3-equivalent for identity-proofing and audit.' The F-04 ASVS mapping reflects this with notations like '6.4.4 (treated as 6.4.6 for L3)' and 'Level 2 → 3'.

expectations.text: Output contains a feature-to-ASVS coverage matrix as a markdown table
expectations.evidence: Section '## Feature ↔ ASVS Coverage Matrix' contains a proper markdown table with columns: Feature, ASVS Section, Requirement ID, Level, Coverage, PRD Change Needed. The table covers all four features and 24 distinct rows mapping ASVS sections (V2.2, V6.x, V7.4, V8.x, V11.4, V12.1, V14.3, V16.x) to specific PRD-change actions.

expectations.text: Output covers all four features F-01, F-02, F-03, and F-04 with the prescribed per-feature shape
expectations.evidence: Each of F-01, F-02, F-03, F-04 has a consistent structure: Actor, Data, Trust Boundaries, ASVS Mapping, Updated Requirements, Acceptance Criteria, Securability Notes. The same headings appear under each feature, and each section is non-trivially populated.

expectations.text: F-01 (forgot password) requires identical response for valid and invalid emails to prevent account enumeration
expectations.evidence: F-01 Updated Requirements: 'The system always returns the same generic success response — same body, same status code, same approximate timing — whether or not the email matches an account, to prevent enumeration (V6.3.8).' Reinforced by an acceptance criterion: 'Submitting a reset request for nobody@example.com (no account) returns the same response body, HTTP status, and response time (within ±50 ms of the median for valid accounts) as a request for an existing account.'

expectations.text: F-01 reset tokens are specified as short-lived (minutes), single-use, and stored as a hash (or equivalent control)
expectations.evidence: F-01: 'Reset tokens are generated using a CSPRNG with at least 128 bits of entropy, are single-use, and expire 15 minutes after issuance. Tokens are stored only as a salted hash; the raw token never persists server-side after delivery (V6.4.1).' Acceptance criteria include rejection at >15 minutes and replay rejection.

expectations.text: F-01 and F-02 flows include rate limiting per email and/or per source IP
expectations.evidence: F-01: 'Reset requests are anti-automated: rate-limited per email address, per source IP, and per CAPTCHA-challenged threshold.' AC: 'More than 5 reset requests for the same email in any 10-minute window result in HTTP 429.' F-02: 'unlock-email requests are rate-limited per account and per source IP.' AC: 'More than 5 unlock-email requests for the same account within 10 minutes returns 429.'

expectations.text: F-03 recovery codes are required to be hashed at rest and invalidated on use
expectations.evidence: F-03: 'Codes are stored at rest only as outputs of an approved password-hashing KDF (Argon2id preferred, bcrypt acceptable) with a per-code random salt' and 'Each code is single-use; on successful redemption the code is marked consumed and cannot be used again.' AC: 'A unit/integration test confirms that retrieving a stored recovery code from the database returns a hash, not plaintext, and that two identical-input generations produce different stored values (proving per-code salt).' AC: 'A recovery code redeemed once is rejected on a second redemption.'

expectations.text: F-04 support-agent bypass requires audit logging and identity verification controls (or dual control)
expectations.evidence: Audit logging: 'Each bypass produces an immutable audit record containing: agent ID, agent session ID, target user ID, UTC timestamp, proofing signals attested, reason category, free-text reason, source IP, and outcome. The audit record is shipped to a logically separate, append-only log store.' Identity verification: 'Identity proofing must be performed at strength equivalent to original enrollment. The verbal challenge alone is insufficient: the agent must collect at least two independent proofing signals from a documented list... Knowledge-based questions like favorite pet are explicitly forbidden.' Plus periodic second-function review of the bypass log (a soft dual-control). RBAC permission 'account.recovery.bypass' is also required.

expectations.text: Acceptance criteria are testable (specific outcomes/observables) rather than aspirational
expectations.evidence: Acceptance criteria across all four features cite specific HTTP statuses (401/403/429), numeric timing tolerances (±50 ms), specific log event names (reset.token.expired, reset.token.replay, reset.ratelimit.email, recovery_codes.lowwatermark), specific test mechanics (DevTools inspection of localStorage/sessionStorage/IndexedDB/cookies; integration test injecting an alt_email field; destructive-action test against a non-prod copy; diffing audit pipeline against expectations). No 'must be secure' or 'should be robust' style language was found.

expectations.text: Output includes a cross-cutting securability requirements section
expectations.evidence: Section '## Cross-Cutting Securability Requirements' enumerates 11 cross-cutting controls: TLS-everywhere, centralized recovery-token module, centralized password-hashing module, structured security logging, anti-automation baseline, generic error responses, SendGrid email channel hygiene, session termination on credential change, user notification on recovery actions, no-KBA, and GDPR/privacy alignment. Each item cites ASVS references.

expectations.text: Output includes an open gaps / assumptions section
expectations.evidence: Section '## Open Gaps and Assumptions' contains 9 documented gaps/assumptions including: support-agent threat model not yet documented; MFA enrollment population unknown; lockout window inherited; email as sole channel limitation; agent identity-proofing signal list not yet drafted; audit retention period unspecified; concurrency/race conditions on token redemption; latency-budget interaction with breached-password screening; localization of notification emails for EU users.
